deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 11-year-old girl with eye issues.
Let me go through the case step by step.
First, the patient presented with redness in her eyes, worse on the right side.
She had some eyelid swelling but no other symptoms like pain or discharge.
She was treated with eye drops but didn't get better.
Then she went to another clinic, got antibiotics, but it didn't help.
Her eye redness persisted, so she was referred to an ophthalmology clinic.
In the ophthalmology exam, they found white cells in the anterior chamber, more on the right.
There were keratic precipitates, posterior synechiae, and rare iris nodules in the right eye.
Funduscopic exam showed optic disk swelling, retinal hemorrhages, and perivascular exudates, especially in the right eye.
They also noted that the pupils were reactive, so that's concerning for uveitis because the pupils should react to light, but if there's inflammation, maybe they're not reacting properly.
Wait, no, the pupils were symmetric and reactive, so that's more normal.
But the other findings are classic for uveitis.
Looking at the lab work, her blood tests were mostly normal except for a high ESR and CRP, which are markers of inflammation.
The CT scan showed mediastinal and axillary lymphadenopathy.
That's concerning for something like tuberculosis or another infectious process.
But the MRI of the orbits showed enhancement of the irises, which is a sign of uveitis.
She was diagnosed with uveitis, treated with steroids and other medications.
Her history includes a positive interferon-gamma release test for TB, which is a sign of latent TB.
She had been prescribed treatment but didn't take it.
So, considering her exposure to TB, maybe she has ocular TB, which can cause uveitis.
The fundus photos showed perivascular exudates and hemorrhages, which are common in uveitis.
Fluorescein angiography showed optic disk leakage, which is another sign.
The MRI of the head showed optic disk protrusion and enhancement, supporting uveitis.
Putting it all together, the key symptoms and findings point to uveitis.
The presence of orbital enhancement on MRI and the history of TB exposure make me think it's probably tuberculous uveitis.
The treatment with anti-TB medications supports this as well.

deepseek-r1-distill-qwen-14b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 11-year-old girl based on the case presentation.
Let me go through the information step by step.
First, the patient presented with redness of the eyes that started 8 weeks ago.
Initially, it was mild and worse in the right eye.
She had some swelling of the eyelids but no photophobia, itching, pain, tearing, or discharge.
Her parents tried naphazoline and glycerin eye drops, which didn't help.
Then she was given an oral antibiotic for preseptal cellulitis, but that didn't work either.
She was referred to an ophthalmologist.
When she was evaluated, her visual acuity was slightly reduced in the left eye, but the pupils were reactive.
Slit-lamp exam showed white cells in the anterior chamber, more in the right eye, along with keratic precipitates, posterior synechiae, and rare iris nodules.
Funduscopic exam revealed optic-disk swelling, retinal hemorrhages, and perivascular exudates, especially in the right eye.
The lab tests showed elevated liver enzymes (AST, ALT), a high ESR, and CRP.
Her ANA and ANCAs were negative.
Imaging showed mediastinal, axillary, and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.
MRI of the head showed optic disk and iris enhancement, consistent with uveitis.
They started her on steroids and anti-tuberculosis meds.
She had a positive interferon-gamma release assay for TB six months prior, but her parents didn't give her the medication for latent TB.
She was on TB treatment now.
Putting this together, the eye symptoms are classic for uveitis.
The presence of anterior chamber inflammation, keratic precipitates, and posterior synechiae support this.
The systemic findings like lymphadenopathy and positive TB test suggest a possible underlying infection, but the eye findings are central.
The extensive retinal involvement, including hemorrhages and exudates, along with optic disk swelling, points towards a severe form of uveitis.
The treatment with steroids and TB meds suggests they're considering TB as a cause, but the primary diagnosis here is the eye inflammation itself.
So, the most likely diagnosis is uveitis, specifically a form that's non-infectious but given the context, it's more about the ocular inflammation itself rather than the underlying cause.

The patient is an 11‑year‑old girl with granulomatous anterior uveitis (keratic precipitates, iris nodules, posterior synechiae), optic‑disc swelling, retinal vasculitis (perivascular exudates, hemorrhages, cotton‑wool spots), and systemic mediastinal, abdominal, and axillary lymphadenopathy.
Her laboratory studies show markedly elevated angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) and lysozyme levels, both of which are classic markers of sarcoidosis.
ANA, ANCA, HIV, syphilis, and Lyme serologies are negative, and there are no systemic features of active tuberculosis (no cough, weight loss, night sweats, or pulmonary infiltrates).
Although the patient has a positive interferon‑γ release assay, this can represent latent TB infection and is not specific for ocular disease in this context.
The constellation of ocular granulomatous inflammation, lymphadenopathy, and elevated ACE/lysozyme is most characteristic of systemic sarcoidosis involving the eyes.
Therefore, the most likely diagnosis is sarcoidosis (ocular sarcoidosis).
<answer> Sarcoidosis </answer>
